content.text: PRR Checklist for Agentic AI Production Services
A
Production Readiness Review (PRR)

is a structured sign-off process that a service or feature must pass before it is allowed to serve real user traffic. A PRR asks: have we thought carefully about every way this system can fail, cause harm, or become unmanageable in production? For conventional software, this is already standard practice at companies like Google, Netflix, and Amazon. Agentic AI services demand a specialized PRR because the failure modes are qualitatively different from ordinary software.
Why Agentic AI Needs a Specialized PRR
Standard PRR checklists were designed for deterministic systems. Agentic AI breaks three assumptions those checklists rely on:
Non-determinism
— the same input does not produce the same output. Evals must run continuously in production, not just at release time.
Tool use side effects
— an agent that calls external APIs, writes to databases, or sends emails can cause real-world consequences that are difficult or impossible to reverse.
Cost unpredictability
— LLM token costs scale with usage in ways that are hard to forecast. A single runaway agent loop can generate thousands of dollars of API spend in minutes.
Safety risks

— LLMs can be manipulated via prompt injection, can produce harmful content, or can be coerced into taking unintended actions. These risks require explicit mitigations that standard PRRs do not address.
Benefits
Catches gaps before production, not after — a PRR is a forcing function that surfaces undocumented failure modes, missing runbooks, and unmonitored cost surfaces.
Creates shared understanding — the PRR review meeting aligns ML engineers, SREs, security, and product on what the system does and what can go wrong.
Establishes accountability — the document is archived and referenced during incidents. It becomes part of the service's institutional memory.
Enables faster incident response — teams that have written failure mode documentation debug production incidents faster because the hypothesis space is already narrowed.
Trade-offs
PRRs take time
— a thorough PRR for a new agentic service takes one to two weeks of preparation and cross-functional review. This is a real cost, justified for services with significant user impact.
Checklists can become theater
— teams under deadline pressure mark items complete without genuine implementation. A PRR is only valuable if the review is honest.
Scope creep is common
— a PRR that tries to address everything risks becoming unwieldy. Scope it to the service boundary and defer lower-risk concerns to follow-up tickets.
How It Works

The PRR process starts when a team declares their service ready for production. An engineer (usually the tech lead) fills out the PRR document — a checklist organized into categories — and then schedules a review meeting with cross-functional stakeholders. Reviewers flag blocking issues that must be resolved before launch and advisory items that should be addressed post-launch. Once all blocking items are cleared, the PRR is signed off and the service is permitted to receive production traffic.
The PRR document is a living artifact. It should be revisited whenever a new model version ships, a major feature is added, or a significant incident occurs.
Architecture

attrs.alt: PRR checklist for agentic AI: eight categories (Reliability, Safety, Observability, Security, Scalability, Operations, Cost, Compliance) with checklist items, followed by the PRR gate flow from draft through reviewer sign-off, staged rollout, and GA launch

content.text: PRR Category 1: Reliability
SLOs defined
— availability, latency (p50 and p99), and error rate targets are documented and agreed upon with stakeholders. LLM calls have long tail latencies; p99 often exceeds p50 by 10x.
Failure modes documented
— what happens when the LLM API is unavailable? When a tool call fails? When the response is malformed? Each failure mode has a documented expected behavior (fallback response, error code, retry).
Retry and circuit breaker in place
— LLM API calls are retried with exponential backoff. A circuit breaker prevents cascading failures when the provider is degraded.
Timeout budgets defined
— every LLM call has a timeout. The timeout is set based on p99 latency plus margin, not an arbitrary large value.
Graceful degradation tested
— the service has been tested in a degraded state (LLM unavailable, slow, or returning errors). Behavior is documented and acceptable.
PRR Category 2: Safety
Prompt injection mitigations
— user input is not concatenated directly into system prompts without sanitization. Injection attack vectors have been tested (e.g., ignore-previous-instructions patterns).
Output filtering
— agent outputs are passed through a guardrail layer (content classifier, regex, or model-based filter) before being shown to users or passed to downstream tools.
Human escalation path
— for high-stakes actions (financial transactions, account deletion, sending bulk communications), the agent must obtain human approval before proceeding.
Tool side-effects bounded
— the maximum scope of any single tool call is explicitly limited (e.g., the email-sending tool can send at most 10 emails per agent run, not unlimited).
Kill switch
— a documented way to disable the agent or any individual tool immediately, without requiring a full service redeployment.
PRR Category 3: Observability
LLM traces

— every LLM call is traced (tool: OpenTelemetry + LLM-aware exporter, or Langfuse/Helicone/Braintrust). Each trace captures: model, prompt version, input token count, output token count, latency, finish reason.
Cost dashboard
— a real-time dashboard shows daily/weekly API spend, cost per request, and cost by tenant or feature. Cost alerts fire if spend exceeds a threshold within a rolling window.
Latency p99 tracked
— p50 and p99 latency for LLM calls and tool calls are tracked and alerted on. A single slow model call can make your entire agent response miss its SLO.
Error rate per tool
— tool call success/failure rates are tracked individually. A single failing tool can silently degrade agent quality without surfacing as a top-level service error.
Agent session replay
— for debugging, you can reconstruct the full sequence of LLM calls, tool calls, and outputs for any agent session from stored traces.
PRR Category 4: Security
Secrets not in prompts
— API keys, database credentials, and PII are never injected into system prompts. An adversarial user who can see model outputs must not be able to extract secrets.
Least-privilege tool permissions
— each tool has only the permissions it needs. A file-reading tool cannot write. A search tool cannot delete. IAM roles and API scopes are tightly scoped.
Audit logging
— every tool call and its arguments are logged with the session ID and user ID. Logs are immutable and stored in a separate system from the application.
Auth on agent APIs
— all endpoints that trigger agent runs require authentication and authorization. Anonymous access is disabled.
No PII in logs
— structured logging scrubs or masks PII fields before writing to log storage. Verify this with a test case that includes a credit card number or email address in agent input.
PRR Category 5: Scalability
Load tested
— the service has been load tested at 2x expected peak traffic. LLM API rate limits were encountered and handled gracefully.
Autoscaling configured
— the service scales horizontally based on queue depth or request rate. Scale-in and scale-out policies have been tuned.
Rate limit handling
— the service handles HTTP 429 responses from the LLM API with backoff and retry. Rate limit headroom has been verified against the provider account limits.
LLM API quota planned
— token-per-minute and request-per-minute quotas have been requested and approved for the expected production traffic level.
PRR Category 6: Operations
Runbooks written
— operational runbooks exist for the most likely failure scenarios: LLM provider outage, runaway cost, agent stuck in a loop, and safety alert firing.
On-call rotation
— a named on-call rotation exists for this service. The rotation is in PagerDuty, OpsGenie, or equivalent. Escalation paths are defined.
Incident response tested
— the team has run a simulated incident (chaos engineering or tabletop exercise) against this service. Response times and runbook accuracy have been evaluated.
Rollback procedure
— a documented and tested rollback procedure exists for model version, system prompt version, and application code independently and in combination.
Change management
— changes to system prompts, model pins, and tool schemas follow the same change management process as code changes (review, staging test, canary rollout).
PRR Category 7: Cost
Token budget per user
— each user or session has a hard or soft token budget. The system enforces the budget and returns a graceful error when the budget is exceeded.
Cost alerts configured
— alerts fire when hourly spend exceeds a threshold. A second alert fires at 80% of the monthly budget.
Spend anomaly detection
— the cost monitoring system detects sudden spikes (e.g., 10x normal spend in 10 minutes) that indicate a runaway loop or abuse pattern.
Cost attribution
— token spend is tagged by tenant, feature, or user tier so cost can be attributed accurately for billing and optimization.
Caching strategy
— repeated identical prompts are served from a semantic or exact cache. Anthropic prompt caching is enabled for long system prompts.
PRR Category 8: Compliance
Data retention policy
— LLM inputs and outputs are retained for a defined period (e.g., 30 days for debugging, 0 days for regulated PII). Retention policy is documented and enforced.
PII handling
— PII that enters agent sessions is identified, and handling rules are documented (anonymize, encrypt at rest, exclude from training data if applicable).
Audit trail for LLM decisions
— for regulated use cases (credit decisions, medical triage, legal advice), every LLM-generated decision that affects a user is logged with the inputs, outputs, and model version.
GDPR / CCPA rights
— there is a documented process to fulfill user data deletion requests for any data stored in agent session logs or eval datasets.
Model usage rights
— the team has reviewed the LLM provider's terms of service and confirmed the intended use case is permitted under the current plan.
How to Implement: PRR Document Template

# prr-template.yaml — Production Readiness Review
# Service: <service name>
# Review date: <YYYY-MM-DD>
# Reviewers: <SRE>, <Security>, <ML Engineer>, <Product>

reliability:
  slos_defined: false          # set to true + link to SLO doc
  failure_modes_documented: false
  retry_circuit_breaker: false
  timeout_budgets: false
  graceful_degradation_tested: false

safety:
  prompt_injection_mitigated: false
  output_filtering: false
  human_escalation_path: false
  tool_side_effects_bounded: false
  kill_switch: false

observability:
  llm_traces: false            # link to trace dashboard
  cost_dashboard: false
  latency_p99_tracked: false
  error_rate_per_tool: false
  session_replay: false

security:
  secrets_not_in_prompts: false
  least_privilege_tools: false
  audit_logging: false
  auth_on_agent_apis: false
  pii_scrubbed_from_logs: false

scalability:
  load_tested: false           # note: at 2x expected peak
  autoscaling_configured: false
  rate_limit_handling: false
  llm_quota_planned: false

operations:
  runbooks_written: false      # link to runbook directory
  oncall_rotation: false
  incident_response_tested: false
  rollback_procedure: false
  change_management: false

cost:
  token_budget_per_user: false
  cost_alerts: false
  anomaly_detection: false
  cost_attribution: false
  caching_strategy: false

compliance:
  data_retention_policy: false
  pii_handling: false
  audit_trail_decisions: false
  gdpr_ccpa_rights: false
  model_usage_rights: false

blocker_items: []
advisory_items: []
sign_off: null
Example: PRR Automation Check Script

# scripts/prr_check.py
# Validates that PRR blockers are cleared before a production deploy.
import yaml
import sys

def check_prr(prr_path: str) -> bool:
    with open(prr_path) as f:
        prr = yaml.safe_load(f)

    categories = [
        "reliability", "safety", "observability", "security",
        "scalability", "operations", "cost", "compliance",
    ]

    # Safety and reliability are blocking categories — must be 100% complete.
    blocking_categories = {"safety", "reliability"}
    failures = []

    for category in categories:
        items = prr.get(category, {})
        incomplete = [k for k, v in items.items() if v is False]
        if incomplete:
            severity = "BLOCKING" if category in blocking_categories else "advisory"
            for item in incomplete:
                failures.append(f"[{severity}] {category}.{item}")

    if not prr.get("sign_off"):
        failures.append("[BLOCKING] PRR has not been signed off by reviewers")

    for failure in failures:
        print(failure)

    blocking = [f for f in failures if "BLOCKING" in f]
    if blocking:
        print(f"\n{len(blocking)} blocking issues must be resolved before production deploy.")
        return False

    advisory = [f for f in failures if "advisory" in f]
    if advisory:
        print(f"{len(advisory)} advisory items — address post-launch.")

    print("PRR check passed.")
    return True

if __name__ == "__main__":
    path = sys.argv[1] if len(sys.argv) > 1 else "prr.yaml"
    sys.exit(0 if check_prr(path) else 1)
Example: Integrating PRR Check into CI/CD

# .github/workflows/deploy.yml
name: Deploy to Production

on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      environment:
        type: choice
        options: [staging, production]

jobs:
  prr-gate:
    if: inputs.environment == 'production'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: PRR completeness check
        run: |
          python scripts/prr_check.py docs/prr/current.yaml
        # Fails the deploy if any BLOCKING PRR item is incomplete

  deploy:
    needs: prr-gate
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Deploy service
        run: ./scripts/deploy.sh production
Real-World Example

A team building an agentic customer support service that can issue refunds, update account settings, and escalate to human agents prepares a PRR three weeks before their planned launch date. During the safety review, the reviewers identify that the refund tool has no upper bound on the refund amount — a prompt injection attack could instruct the agent to issue a $10,000 refund. This becomes a blocking issue. The team adds a hard limit of $500 per agent-initiated refund and adds a mandatory human approval step for amounts above $50. The cost review finds that no token budget is set and a single aggressive user could generate $200 in API spend in an afternoon session. Token budgets per session (soft at 50k tokens, hard at 100k tokens) are added. These two blockers are resolved in a week; the PRR is signed off and the service launches on schedule with documented mitigations for both risks.
Common Mistakes
Treating the PRR as a checkbox exercise
— marking items complete without genuine implementation. Reviewers should ask for evidence (links to dashboards, test results, runbook URLs) not just a boolean flag.
Skipping the PRR for small features
— a new tool added to an existing agent can introduce significant side-effect surface area. PRR scope should include any change that expands the agent's capabilities.
No re-review after model upgrades
— a new model version can change safety properties, latency characteristics, and cost. Treat model version upgrades as a PRR trigger, not a routine config change.
Incomplete failure mode analysis
— teams document the happy path but skip what happens when a tool call fails mid-session, when the LLM returns an empty response, or when two concurrent sessions conflict on a shared resource.
No cost ceiling
— launching without a per-user or per-session token budget is the most common agentic AI cost incident pattern. Always set a hard ceiling.
Best Practices
Start the PRR early
— begin filling in the PRR document two to three weeks before the target launch date. Blocking issues discovered one day before launch are painful.
Assign a PRR owner
— one engineer is responsible for driving the PRR to completion. Without a named owner, PRR items fall through the cracks.
Automate the compliance check
— gate production deploys on a CI step that parses the PRR YAML and fails if blocking items are incomplete or the document is unsigned.
Archive signed PRRs

— store completed PRR documents in a version-controlled repository. During an incident, the PRR is often the fastest way to understand what failure modes were anticipated and what mitigations were in place.
Review the PRR after every major incident
— if an incident reveals a failure mode that was not in the PRR, update the PRR and add a new item to the template for future services.
Performance and Scalability

The PRR process itself does not affect service performance, but the items it requires do. LLM tracing adds a small overhead per request (typically 1–5ms for asynchronous span export). Output filtering adds latency proportional to the complexity of the filter; model-based classifiers add 100–500ms. Budget these costs into your SLO before the PRR.

For high-throughput agentic services, the scalability category of the PRR should include a specific load test result: what is the measured throughput (requests per second), p99 latency, and LLM API error rate at 2x peak load? Document these numbers in the PRR rather than leaving them implicit.
Tooling and Ecosystem
Langfuse / Helicone / Braintrust
— LLM observability platforms that provide the traces, cost dashboards, and eval tracking required by the observability category.
Guardrails AI / NeMo Guardrails
— output filtering frameworks for the safety category.
AWS AppConfig / LaunchDarkly
— dynamic config and feature flags for kill switches and partial rollbacks (operations category).
PagerDuty / OpsGenie
— on-call rotation and alert routing (operations category).
Notion / Confluence / GitHub
— PRR document storage and version control. Many teams maintain PRR documents as checked-in YAML in their service repository.
Comparison with Alternatives
Approach
Thoroughness
Time Cost
Best For
Agentic AI PRR (full)
High
1–2 weeks
New agentic services with real-world side effects
Lightweight PRR (safety + ops only)
Medium
2–3 days
Minor feature additions to existing agents
Standard SWE PRR
Low (for AI)
1 day
Deterministic services without LLM or tool use
Ad-hoc review
Variable
Hours
Prototypes and internal tools only
No review
None
None
Not recommended for any production agentic service
Security and Reliability
Gate production deploys on PRR sign-off
— a CI/CD check that parses the PRR YAML and blocks the deploy if blocking items are unsigned or incomplete is the most reliable enforcement mechanism.
Require cross-functional reviewers
— an SRE, a security engineer, and an ML engineer must all sign off. A PRR signed only by the development team has systematic blind spots.
Treat the PRR as a living document
— a PRR signed six months ago is stale. Re-review after significant changes (new tools, new model, new compliance requirements).
Mental Model: PRR as a Pre-Flight Checklist

Pilots do not rely on memory before takeoff — they run a checklist every time, for every flight, regardless of experience level. The value is not that experienced pilots do not know the items; it is that the checklist makes forgetting impossible. A PRR for an agentic AI service serves the same function. The cost of running through it is an hour of review. The cost of skipping it can be a safety incident, a runaway cost event, or a compliance violation that takes weeks to resolve.
Key Takeaways
A PRR is a structured sign-off checklist run before any new agentic service or major feature reaches production.
Agentic AI requires a specialized PRR covering eight categories: Reliability, Safety, Observability, Security, Scalability, Operations, Cost, and Compliance.
Safety and cost categories are the highest-risk for agentic services — prompt injection, unbounded tool side-effects, and runaway token spend are the most common production incidents.
Gate production deploys on PRR completion using a CI/CD script that parses the PRR document and blocks the pipeline if blocking items are incomplete.
Require cross-functional reviewers — SRE, security, and ML engineers each catch distinct categories of risk that a single team cannot.
Treat the PRR as a living document — re-review after model version upgrades, new tool additions, and post-incident.
Archive signed PRRs in version control — they become the primary reference during incidents for understanding what failure modes were anticipated and what mitigations were in place.
